talk_title: Pre‑cancer evolution of the upper gastrointestinal tract using organoid and single‑cell models

targets.dependency_context: intestinal metaplasia organoids vs gastric organoids

biomarkers.name: MSI
biomarkers.finding: MSI subtype responds to PD‑1 inhibition in gastric cancer

biomarkers.name: PD‑1 high tumor
biomarkers.finding: PD‑1 high tumors show response to anti‑PD‑1 therapy

biomarkers.name: Claudin‑18.2 expression
biomarkers.finding: Reduced expression in metaplastic organoids suggests a potential escape mechanism

models.name: Gastric and intestinal metaplasia organoid biobank
models.genotype_or_key_features: includes ALK fusion, RHOA mutation, EBV+, diffuse type, chromosome 20 gain

models.name: microdissected gastric gland crypts
models.genotype_or_key_features: 200+ whole‑genome sequenced glands, age‑related SNV accumulation

MOA_summary: Deep whole‑genome sequencing of microdissected gastric glands revealed age‑related SNV accumulation and a two‑fold SNV/three‑fold indel increase in intestinal metaplasia glands, indicating clonal expansion from a common ancestor. Single‑cell RNA‑seq of organoids shows a spectrum from hybrid gastric‑intestinal phenotypes to advanced intestinal differentiation, driven by epigenetic reprogramming (hypermethylation of gastric genes, demethylation of colon genes) and fetal signature reversion, with chromosome 20 gain linked to matrix‑independent growth.
key_findings: Microdissected metaplastic glands display ~2‑fold higher SNV burden and ~3‑fold higher indel rate than adjacent non‑metaplastic glands.
Mutational signature SBS18 (ROS injury) and increased ID2/ID1 ratio emerge early in metaplasia, resembling MSI tumor signatures.
Single‑cell RNA‑seq defines hybrid gastric‑intestinal cells and a fetal gut signature (TROP2, TACSTD2) in metaplastic organoids.
Chromosome 20 gain is frequent in metaplastic organoids and enables growth without Matrigel, suggesting an early tumor‑initiating event.
Claudin‑18.2 expression declines during metaplastic progression, potentially mediating resistance to CLDN18.2‑targeted therapies.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Loss of CLDN18.2 expression in advanced metaplastic organoids may represent an escape mechanism from CLDN18.2‑targeted antibodies.
open_questions: Can chromosome 20 gain be used as a predictive biomarker for progression to invasive gastric cancer?
What molecular events trigger the transition from hybrid to fully intestinal metaplasia?
How can the fetal reversion signature be therapeutically targeted to prevent tumor initiation?

talk_title: HER2‑directed bispecific antibody zanidatamab in metastatic gastric/GEJ cancer

targets.dependency_context: HER2‑positive gastric/GEJ cancer (3+ IHC or 2+ with ERBB2 amplification)

biomarkers.name: HER2 overexpression/amplification
biomarkers.finding: Patients must be HER2‑positive (3+ IHC or 2+ with ERBB2 copy number gain) to enroll

models.name: Horizon GEA‑01
models.genotype_or_key_features: Arm A: trastuzumab + chemo; Arm B: zanidatamab + chemo; Arm C: zanidatamab + tislelizumab + chemo; HER2‑positive GEA, ~10‑12% prevalence

clinical_data.population: zanidatamab + tislelizumab + chemo vs trastuzumab + chemo

MOA_summary: Zanidatamab is an asymmetric bispecific antibody that simultaneously engages two HER2 epitopes, inducing receptor clustering, cap formation and hexamerization, which markedly enhances FcγR cross‑linking, complement C1q recruitment and thereby drives ADCC, ADCP and complement‑dependent cytotoxicity beyond that achieved by trastuzumab, pertuzumab or their combination.
key_findings: Zanidatamab induces HER2 cap formation and hexamerization not observed with trastuzumab, pertuzumab, or their combination.
In the fourth‑line setting, zanidatamab monotherapy produced durable responses absent with standard HER2‑targeted therapy.
Phase II zanidatamab + chemo yielded a median PFS advantage of 4 months over trastuzumab + chemo (HR 0.65, p < 0.001).
Adding tislelizumab to zanidatamab + chemo further improved OS (HR 0.72, ~7‑month gain) compared with trastuzumab + chemo.
Diarrhea was the most common toxicity in the zanidatamab‑chemo arms, requiring proactive management.

combination_strategies.rationale: Chemotherapy provides cytotoxic backbone while zanidatamab enhances HER2‑mediated immune effector functions.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Four‑month PFS improvement and seven‑month OS gain versus trastuzumab‑chemo

combination_strategies.rationale: Combining HER2 bispecific with PD‑1 blockade may synergize immune activation and overcome resistance.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Further OS benefit (HR 0.72) over chemotherapy + trastuzumab

open_questions: Will zanidatamab benefit HER2‑low (2+ without amplification) gastric cancers?
What biomarkers predict response beyond HER2 IHC/FISH status?
Can earlier‑line use of zanidatamab improve cure rates in resectable disease?

talk_title: Neoadjuvant chemo‑immunotherapy (FLOT + durvalumab) reshapes curative treatment of resectable gastroesophageal cancer

biomarkers.name: PD‑L1 CPS
biomarkers.finding: Patients with CPS ≥1 received benefit from durvalumab‑FLOT

biomarkers.name: Major pathologic response (MPR)
biomarkers.finding: MPR (<10% residual tumor) observed in 19% of patients and correlated with long‑term survival

biomarkers.name: Complete pathologic response (pCR)
biomarkers.finding: pCR achieved in 19% of durvalumab‑FLOT arm

models.name: Matterhorn (Phase III)
models.genotype_or_key_features: FLOT + durvalumab vs FLOT + placebo in resectable HER2‑negative GEA, global enrollment

MOA_summary: Durvalumab combined with the triplet chemotherapy FLOT delivers sustained neo‑antigen release and checkpoint blockade while the tumor remains in situ, leading to deep tumor regression (MPR/pCR) and enhanced immune priming that enables organ‑preserving strategies in resectable gastroesophageal adenocarcinoma.
key_findings: Neoadjuvant durvalumab + FLOT achieved a 19% pCR rate and 19% MPR rate, markedly higher than historic chemotherapy alone.
Patients with pCR had durable disease‑free survival with median follow‑up >4 years.
Clinical complete response was observed in 83% of early‑cohort patients after six cycles of neoadjuvant therapy.
ctDNA clearance correlated with pathologic response but 38% of ctDNA‑negative patients still recurred, highlighting residual risk.
Chromosomal instability (CIN) signatures predicted poorer immunotherapy benefit, suggesting a need for additional biomarkers.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: CIN tumors showed reduced benefit from durvalumab‑FLOT, likely due to cGAS‑STING mediated immune evasion.

combination_strategies.rationale: Chemotherapy provides tumor antigen release and cytoreduction while durvalumab blocks PD‑L1 to sustain T‑cell activity.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Improved pCR/MPR rates and prolonged OS in Phase III Matterhorn

combination_strategies.rationale: Dual checkpoint blockade may overcome resistance in CIN‑high tumors.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Exploratory data suggest additive OS benefit when combined with durvalumab‑FLOT

open_questions: What molecular or immune signatures can reliably identify patients who will achieve pCR and may be candidates for non‑operative management?
How can CIN‑driven cGAS‑STING suppression be therapeutically reversed to improve immunotherapy response?
What is the optimal duration of neoadjuvant immunotherapy to balance efficacy and toxicity?
